text: The video displays the placement of five trocars for the laparoscopic procedure. The camera port is positioned above the umbilicus, while two operating trocars are located in the lower and upper right quadrants. Two additional trocars are placed in the left flank near the pubic region to assist with rectal retraction.
The surgeon makes a peristomal incision to mobilize the terminal ileostomy, which is then closed using a linear stapler.

An Alexis retractor is positioned at the previous ileostomy site, facilitating the creation of pneumoperitoneum. The surgeon demonstrates the reach of the apex of the J-pouch, marking its position on the abdominal skin before performing any elongation maneuvers.
Inside the abdomen, the surgeon performs uteropexy to enhance pelvic exposure. The rectal stump is identified and stitched to facilitate effective rectal traction and counter traction.

The surgeon begins a nerve-sparing rectal dissection from the posterior plane, seeking the avascular plane between the pre-sacral fascia and the mesorectal fascia, using techniques similar to those in rectal cancer surgery.
The surgeon continues the dissection down to the pelvic floor, revealing the puborectalis muscle, which is visible as the dissection progresses deeper into the pelvic cavity.

The surgeon performs lateral and anterior mobilization, focusing on a precise rectal dissection to minimize the risk of injuring autonomic pelvic nerves. The anterior dissection is visibly developed behind the rectovaginal septum along the rectal muscularis propria.
The surgeon uses a 45mm linear stapler with a double cartridge to staple the rectum approximately two centimeters above the dentine line after fully skeletonizing the anorectal junction.
The surgeon positions themselves between the patient's legs to fully mobilize the small bowel mesentery, extending the dissection up to its origin at the duodenum as part of the elongation maneuver.
The surgeon exteriorizes the small bowel, demonstrating a gain in length of three centimeters following intracorporeal ileal mobilization.
The surgeon removes mesenteric fat between the ileocolic vessels and the terminal branches of the superior mesenteric vessels, gaining an additional centimeter in length of the small bowel.
The surgeon performs a mesenteric serosal incision perpendicular to the mesenteric axis on both sides of the mesentery, as part of the third elongation technique.

The surgeon demonstrates a five-centimeter elongation at the apex of the pouch, achieved through a series of maneuvers including mesenteric serosal incision, removal of mesenteric fat, and full mobilization of the small bowel mesentery.

The surgeon tests the blood supply at the apex of the pouch using a vascular clamp before ligating the terminal part of the superior mesenteric vessels, demonstrating an additional three centimeters of elongation in a patient with short mesentery.
The surgeon fashions an ileal J pouch, approximately one centimeter in length, using multiple firings of a linear stapler to create the pouch structure.

Following the reinduction of pneumoperitoneum, the surgeon performs an ileal pouch-anal anastomosis using a 29mm circular stapler. A diverting loop ileostomy is created, and a pelvic drain is placed to complete the operation.
